opus_warpgrep.text: The totpStep1 key contains Italian text 'Installa una delle seguenti applicazioni sul tuo cellulare:' in the Lithuanian (lt) locale file. All surrounding keys are in Lithuanian. This is a wrong-language copy-paste error — Lithuanian users will see Italian text instead of Lithuanian for the TOTP setup step 1 message.

The totpStep1 key uses Traditional Chinese characters '在您的手機上安裝以下應用程式之一：' (手機, 安裝, 應用程式) in the Simplified Chinese (zh_CN/zh_Hans) locale file. Surrounding keys use Simplified Chinese (e.g., line before: mobile=手机). The correct Simplified Chinese should use 手机, 安装, 应用程序. This is a wrong-variant substitution — Simplified Chinese users will see Traditional Chinese text.

Copy-paste bug: `Objects.requireNonNull(grantType, "Null rawTokenId not allowed")` validates `grantType` a second time instead of validating `rawTokenId`. The parameter `rawTokenId` is never null-checked, so a null `rawTokenId` can be stored in the field and will cause a NullPointerException downstream when `getRawTokenId()` is used in `encodeTokenId()` (which concatenates it via `':' + tokenContext.getRawTokenId()`). The fix is to change `grantType` to `rawTokenId` on this line.

Two bugs on this line in `isAccessTokenId` matcher: (1) Inverted condition: `if (items[0].substring(3, 5).equals(expectedGrantShortcut)) return false` returns false when the grant shortcut MATCHES, but it should return false when it does NOT match. The condition should use `!equals`. (2) Wrong substring indices: `substring(3, 5)` extracts characters at indices 3-4, but the grant type shortcut is at indices 4-5 in the 6-char prefix (format is `[sessionType:0-1][tokenType:2-3][grantType:4-5]:rawId`, confirmed in `DefaultTokenContextEncoderProvider.getTokenContextFromTokenId` which uses `substring(4, 6)`). Should be `if (!items[0].substring(4, 6).equals(expectedGrantShortcut)) return false`. Together these bugs make the matcher completely ignore the grant type check and accept any 6-char prefix.

In `getCredentials()`, the `RecoveryAuthnCodesCredentialModel` created via `createFromValues()` does NOT have its ID set (`createFromValues` never calls `setId`). However, the stored `myUser.recoveryCodes` does have an ID (set via `KeycloakModelUtils.generateId()` in `updateCredential` at line 196). This ID is not propagated to the returned model. In contrast, OTP credentials at line 249 return `myUser.getOtp()` directly, which preserves the ID. When this null-ID credential is returned through `getFederatedCredentialsStream()` and used in `RecoveryAuthnCodesFormAuthenticator.isRecoveryAuthnCodeInputValid()`, `createFromCredentialModel` copies the null ID. If `allCodesUsed()` ever returned true, `removeStoredCredentialById(null)` would be called, causing NPE or incorrect behavior. Fix: add `model.setId(myUser.recoveryCodes.getId())` after `createFromValues()`.

The `isValid()` for recovery codes uses `generatedKeys.stream().anyMatch(key -> key.equals(input.getChallengeResponse()))`, which accepts ANY stored recovery code as valid. The production `RecoveryAuthnCodesCredentialProvider.isValid()` only validates the NEXT unused code in sequence (via `getNextRecoveryAuthnCode()` + `verifyRecoveryCodeInput` with hash comparison). This means the test storage accepts out-of-order codes and allows code reuse (since codes are never consumed from storage), which doesn't match production behavior and could mask bugs in sequential code validation for federated users.

NullPointerException in `canMapRoles(ClientModel client)` when `client` is `null`. This method delegates directly to `hasPermission(client, AdminPermissionsSchema.MAP_ROLES)`, and the private `hasPermission(ClientModel, String)` method accesses `client.getId()` without a null check. The same issue affects `canMapCompositeRoles(null)` and `canMapClientScopeRoles(null)`. The existing caller `AvailableRoleMappingResource.canPerformOnAllClients()` explicitly calls `auth.clients().canMapRoles(null)`, `canMapCompositeRoles(null)`, and `canMapClientScopeRoles(null)`. This will crash at runtime with an NPE for any user without the `MANAGE_CLIENTS` admin role when V2 fine-grained permissions are enabled. The `hasPermission` method needs a null check on `client` and should fall back to evaluating against the all-clients resource type (similar to how the no-arg `hasPermission(String scope)` method works).

Javadoc copy-paste error: the Javadoc for `requireView()` (a `void` method) says "Returns {@code true} if {@link #canView()} returns {@code true}." It should say "Throws ForbiddenException if {@link #canView()} returns {@code false}." — matching the pattern used for other `require*` methods like `requireManage()` and `requireList()`. The same copy-paste error exists for `requireViewClientScopes()` at line 104.

GroupPermissionsV2.canManage() (no-arg) checks `hasPermission(null, AdminPermissionsSchema.VIEW, AdminPermissionsSchema.MANAGE)` — identical to canView(). Including the VIEW scope means a user who only has VIEW permission on groups will pass the manage check, which is a privilege escalation. The fgap version of the same method (services/src/main/java/org/keycloak/services/resources/admin/fgap/GroupPermissionsV2.java:64) correctly only checks `AdminPermissionsSchema.MANAGE`. This is a copy-paste error from canView(). Consequence: view-only users can create, update, and delete groups via `requireManage()` guards in GroupsResource.addTopLevelGroup(), GroupResource.updateGroup(), deleteGroup(), and addChild().

In getGroupIdsWithViewPermission(), `groupResource.getId()` is used in both `hasPermission(groupResource.getId(), ...)` and `granted.add(groupResource.getId())`. However, `getId()` returns the authorization resource's auto-generated internal ID, NOT the group model's ID. The resource name (set during creation via `resourceStore.create(resourceServer, name, ...)` where name = group model ID) is what contains the actual group ID. The parent class GroupPermissions correctly uses `resource.getName().substring(RESOURCE_NAME_PREFIX.length())`. Both occurrences should use `groupResource.getName()`. Consequence: `hasPermission` will always fall through to the type-level resource check (since `resourceStore.findByName(server, autoGeneratedId)` won't match), and the returned set will contain wrong IDs that won't match any group models during user search filtering.

Dead code: `ASN1Encoder.create().write(rBigInteger)` and `ASN1Encoder.create().write(sBigInteger)` on lines 115-116 create ASN1Encoder instances, encode the BigInteger values, and the returned encoder objects are immediately discarded. The actual DER encoding is correctly done on lines 118-122 via `writeDerSeq`. These two lines perform wasted computation and appear to be leftover from an incomplete refactor.

The new `order()` method is added to the `CryptoProvider` interface without a `default` implementation. Since `CryptoProvider` is a public interface, any third-party implementations that don't add the `order()` method will fail at runtime with `AbstractMethodError` when `detectProvider()` calls `Comparator.comparingInt(CryptoProvider::order)` during ServiceLoader discovery. Adding `default int order() { return 0; }` would maintain backward compatibility.

getSubGroupsCount() now returns null when modelSupplier.get() returns null, violating the GroupModel interface contract which explicitly states '@return The number of groups beneath this group. Never returns {@code null}.' (GroupModel.java line 310). All callers (GroupUtils.populateSubGroupCount, OrganizationGroupResource, OrganizationGroupsResource, GroupResource.getSubGroups) pass the result to GroupRepresentation.setSubGroupCount(Long) which accepts boxed Long so they won't immediately NPE, but any caller that auto-unboxes to primitive `long` (e.g., `long count = group.getSubGroupsCount()`) will throw NullPointerException. The fix should return 0L instead of null to maintain the interface contract.

The reader Thread started at line 143 is never join()ed before the assertion at line 161. After deletedAll.set(true), the reader thread may still be mid-HTTP-request (it entered the try block before the loop condition was re-checked). If that in-flight request fails, the exception is added to caughtExceptions AFTER the main thread's assertThat has already passed. This means the test can give a false pass — the race condition it's designed to detect could still throw server-side errors that are silently missed. The Thread reference should be stored and join()ed between deletedAll.set(true) and the assertion.

The call `isConditionalPasskeysEnabled()` with zero arguments will fail to compile. The only definition of this method is `isConditionalPasskeysEnabled(UserModel user)` in the parent class UsernamePasswordForm, which requires one argument. No zero-argument overload exists anywhere in the class hierarchy (UsernameForm → UsernamePasswordForm → AbstractUsernameFormAuthenticator → AbstractFormAuthenticator). Grepping the entire codebase confirms only one definition with `UserModel` parameter. This should be `isConditionalPasskeysEnabled(context.getUser())`.

The new `isConditionalPasskeysEnabled` method requires `user != null`, but the old code in `challenge(context, error, field)` that it replaces had the opposite condition: `context.getUser() == null && webauthnAuth != null && webauthnAuth.isPasskeysEnabled()`. The old code set up passkeys when the user was NOT yet identified (initial login error page). The new code with `user != null` only sets up passkeys when the user IS identified (re-authentication), completely inverting the behavior. This breaks passkeys display on initial login error pages. The condition should be `(user == null || user.credentialManager().isConfiguredFor(webauthnAuth.getCredentialType()))` instead of `user != null`.

Inconsistent metric tag key: `tags={"shards": shard_tag}` (plural) on the `spans.buffer.flusher.wait_produce` timer, while the adjacent metrics on lines 309 and 319 use `tags={"shard": shard_tag}` (singular). This tag name mismatch means dashboard queries filtering by `shard` won't include the `wait_produce` timer data (which uses `shards`), and vice versa. Should be `"shard"` to match the other metrics in the same function.

The added `time.sleep(0.1)` on this line is a no-op because `monkeypatch.setattr("time.sleep", lambda _: None)` earlier in the test replaces `time.sleep` with a lambda that does nothing. The comment says 'Give flusher threads time to process after drift change' but the sleep has no effect. The test then immediately calls `step.join()` which sets `stopped.value = True`, potentially stopping the flusher thread before it has a chance to process the drift change and flush segments. This makes the test racy — it may pass or fail depending on thread scheduling.

The `isinstance(process, multiprocessing.Process)` check was added to guard `process.kill()`, but processes are created via `self.mp_context.Process` (spawn context), which returns `multiprocessing.context.SpawnProcess`. `SpawnProcess` inherits from `multiprocessing.process.BaseProcess`, NOT from `multiprocessing.context.Process` (which is what `multiprocessing.Process` refers to). They are sibling classes. This means the isinstance check is False for SpawnProcess instances, so `process.kill()` is never called — a regression from the old code which called `.kill()` unconditionally. Unhealthy processes won't be killed before being restarted, potentially causing duplicate processes running on the same shards. The type annotation `dict[int, multiprocessing.context.SpawnProcess | threading.Thread]` confirms SpawnProcess is the expected type. Consider using `not isinstance(process, threading.Thread)` or `isinstance(process, multiprocessing.process.BaseProcess)` instead.
